title: Old Start, An

jokes.text: Now the story of a wealthy family who lost everything, and the one son who had no choice but to keep them all together.
jokes.setup: The show's iconic opening narration, repeated every episode
jokes.punchline: The irony that Michael is now president of the company and clearly not keeping anything together — escalated by the episode's chaos
jokes.explanation: The show's signature opening lands differently each episode as the situational irony deepens. By Season 5 the comedy is almost entirely meta — the audience knows exactly what they're in for. Strong economy and structure but docked for not being new.

jokes.text: I was part of that Cornballer class action suit. I made enough to put a down payment on a Sitwell.
jokes.setup: Man says the Bluth Company put him in his first house
jokes.punchline: He won a class action lawsuit from the Cornballer — a dangerous appliance from the Bluth family — and used the settlement to buy a Sitwell home (the rival company)
jokes.explanation: A beautifully compressed callback that rewards longtime viewers. The Cornballer paying for a Sitwell home is elegant poetic justice. The joke does double duty: mocks the Bluths and crowns the rival in one breath. High earned score because it requires genuine show knowledge to land.

jokes.text: Well, we've put a lot of people in Sitwells, too.
jokes.setup: Worker says Cornballer money funded his Sitwell home
jokes.punchline: Michael, without missing a beat, pivots to treating it as a sales accomplishment — the Bluths indirectly fill Sitwell homes through their incompetence
jokes.explanation: Michael's deadpan reframe is perfectly in character — he unconsciously spins every embarrassment into corporate-speak. The line is economical and the character integration is strong. A genuinely clever beat.

jokes.text: Didn't think anybody would be getting on while we were getting off.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is surprised to be caught in the elevator
jokes.punchline: Innocent elevator language doubles as accidental sexual innuendo, delivered with G.O.B.'s characteristic obliviousness
jokes.explanation: A mild double entendre that works because G.O.B. genuinely doesn't know what he's saying. Not especially original but solid execution.

jokes.text: An iPhone 5 charger! Fourth floor.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is asked what he did to the Bluth Company in two weeks as president
jokes.punchline: He announces the office upgrade is an iPhone 5 charger — already an outdated model at time of airing — as if it's a revolutionary innovation, and specifies the floor
jokes.explanation: Perfectly encapsulates G.O.B.'s executive incompetence — his greatest achievement is a charging cable, and he's proud of it. The specificity of 'Fourth floor' adds a layer of absurdist bureaucratic pride.

jokes.text: Just to see the roof of a Rite Aid? Which was the first that G.O.B. had heard of the view.
jokes.setup: Michael complains about the enormous cost of the new office lease just to see a rooftop
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals G.O.B. hadn't even realized there was a view — he moved the entire company for reasons that didn't include the view he supposedly loves
jokes.explanation: The narrator's aside is a model of the AD formula: character does something inexplicably stupid, narrator undercuts it further. The realization that G.O.B. moved a whole company for no reason he can articulate — and then immediately loves the view — is excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: It used to be a 2 Hour Teeth, the whole place. Yeah, I remember the screams.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. mentions the office space used to be a 2 Hour Teeth dental chain
jokes.punchline: Michael casually remembers 'the screams' as if rapid dentistry is a naturally horrifying operation — delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: Short, dark, and perfectly economical. The idea that 2-hour dentistry is so terrible you'd remember the screams years later is inspired dark comedy. Michael's deadpan delivery makes it land.

jokes.text: I guess it was the 90 Minute Tooth guys across the street that put the 2 Hour guys out of business. I did see that the old Blockbuster is becoming an 'Hour Mouth,' so those guys gotta be shitting themselves.
jokes.setup: The competitive dental chain market is introduced as a running background joke
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. tracks the competitive landscape of rapid dental chains as if it's a normal industry to follow — culminating in 'Hour Mouth' threatening to disrupt again, and his genuine concern for the 90-minute guys
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The absurd premise — a cutthroat speed-dentistry industry operating on pure time-compression competition — is escalated brilliantly. Referencing Blockbuster (already a joke in itself, cultural reference to dead video chain) being replaced by 'Hour Mouth' is an inspired left turn. G.O.B.'s genuine competitive concern for the 90-minute guys is the character-perfect cherry on top. High originality because the concept is genuinely invented and internally consistent.

jokes.text: Says the guy who hasn't seen the roof of the Rite Aid yet.
jokes.setup: Michael says the new office is 'exactly the same' as the old one
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. defends the move entirely on the basis of the Rite Aid roof view — which he only just learned about — as if it's the obvious answer to all objections
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback to the narrator's reveal that G.O.B. didn't know about the view. Him now weaponizing it as his primary defense is perfectly G.O.B. — he adopts justifications retroactively with full confidence.

jokes.text: Boy, what's a bike tire doing on the roof of the Rite Aid? Yeah, that's the $14,000 a month question, isn't it?
jokes.setup: G.O.B. invites Michael to check out the amazing view
jokes.punchline: The view reveals only a random bike tire on a rooftop — the entire expensive lease justified by a mundane, confusing sight
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the Rite Aid view buildup is excellent: not a dramatic view but a single inexplicable bike tire. Michael's '$14,000 a month question' reframe is sardonic gold. The visual gag (inferred) of a bike tire on a roof perfectly embodies the show's anti-climax comedy.

jokes.text: When they said you were the president of the Bluth Company, I assumed it was in name only. That they hired you to actually work seems wrong. Do you even know what a mortgage is?
jokes.setup: Adhir, the compliance officer, observes G.O.B. leading the company
jokes.punchline: He openly expresses disbelief that G.O.B. has actual responsibilities, then questions whether he knows what a mortgage is — a fundamental industry concept
jokes.explanation: A solid character burn that works because Adhir is so blunt. The escalation from 'in name only' to 'do you know what a mortgage is' is well-structured. Not especially original but well-executed.

jokes.text: No, he doesn't. He doesn't know anything. You do, though. You're the smart Bluth, I can see it in your eyes. You read, don't you?
jokes.setup: Adhir dismisses G.O.B. and turns to Michael
jokes.punchline: He identifies Michael as 'the smart Bluth' with the bar-setting distinction of 'You read, don't you?' — reading being the minimum threshold for intelligence
jokes.explanation: 'You read, don't you?' is a quietly inspired line — the implication that basic literacy is what distinguishes the smartest Bluth is a perfect encapsulation of the family's intellectual level. Delivered deadpan, it lands as both a compliment and an insult.

jokes.text: That's it, go to your office, Adhir. He's the chief compliant officer, so he has to do whatever I comply.
jokes.setup: Earlier established that Adhir is 'compliance officer,' which G.O.B. called 'Chief Worrywart'
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. mangles 'compliance' into 'compliant' — meaning someone who must obey — and constructs a sentence where HE is the verb ('whatever I comply'), confident in his complete misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout jokes. G.O.B.'s word corruption is not random — it follows a consistent internal logic where he has genuinely mistaken the role as 'someone who complies with ME.' The sentence 'he has to do whatever I comply' is grammatically G.O.B.-perfect: he's even made himself the subject of compliance. High character integration because this exact brand of confident malapropism is core G.O.B. The callback structure elevates it further.

jokes.text: I'm assuming that's where the conference table went. It's probably too big for that room, and I would get it out, but I can't quite remember how I got it in there.
jokes.setup: Michael observes there's no conference room
jokes.punchline: He reveals the conference table is trapped in an office he can't figure out how to remove it from — echoing classic AD physical comedy logic (things getting trapped in spaces, like the stair car)
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist gag. The mystery of how a table got into a room is a solid but not remarkable joke. Competent filler.

jokes.text: Dad is giving a trophy to Lindsay, not me, for the family of the year ceremony tonight.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. wants to know what thanks he's getting for running the company
jokes.punchline: His complaint is that he's not getting a trophy — revealing his motivation is entirely about parental approval in the form of a physical award
jokes.explanation: Character-consistent joke about G.O.B.'s deep need for paternal validation. Sets up the trophy subplot. Functional but not sharp.

jokes.text: I will get a trophy for Dad, for... something. Figure that out later.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. declares he'll win a trophy
jokes.punchline: He has no idea what the trophy will be for — 'for something' — and dismisses the problem as something to solve later, committing to the goal before the plan
jokes.explanation: 'Figure that out later' is the G.O.B. life philosophy in three words. Strong character integration — he commits fully to half-formed plans. The pause before 'something' is the joke.

jokes.text: Then why did you move into your son's dorm room? That was a year, okay? And not even a full year, because I got there at Thanksgiving.
jokes.setup: George Sr. philosophizes about fathers giving sons space
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately counters with George Sr. having moved into Michael's son's dorm room — and George Sr.'s defense is that it was only one year, AND he arrived late (Thanksgiving)
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent joke. The structure is perfect: philosophical setup → devastating specific rebuttal → defensive mitigation that makes it worse. 'Not even a full year, because I got there at Thanksgiving' is the kind of detail that makes AD great — the specificity of the Thanksgiving arrival as a defense is absurd and character-perfect. George Sr. treating 'I arrived late' as meaningful mitigation for moving into his grandson's dorm room is hilarious. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor example in structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Uh... George Michael? Father. It's me, George Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael calls for his son in what he thinks is George Michael's bedroom
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds 'Father. It's me, George Michael' — he has dressed as George Michael and is doing 'character work' in the kid's bedroom
jokes.explanation: Strong setup for the Tobias-as-George-Michael bit. The reveal of Tobias doing 'character work' in a teenager's bedroom is appropriately creepy and funny. Good character integration for Tobias's method actor delusions.

jokes.text: Not only are you finding me in your son's pants, but you're also finding out how the sausage is made.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers Tobias in George Michael's room wearing George Michael's clothes
jokes.punchline: 'In your son's pants' is simultaneously literal (wearing his trousers) and horrifyingly suggestive. 'Finding out how the sausage is made' adds a second layer of unintentional innuendo about the acting process.
jokes.explanation: A standout Tobias line. 'In your son's pants' is the best kind of AD double meaning — completely literal within context (the costume) and simultaneously horrifying. Adding 'how the sausage is made' compounds the unintentional innuendo perfectly. This is Tobias at his most Tobias: a completely innocent explanation that sounds maximally inappropriate. High character integration — this joke cannot exist without Tobias's specific brand of oblivious self-expression.

jokes.text: I don't understand a thing you said.
jokes.setup: Tobias delivers his pants/sausage line
jokes.punchline: Michael's total bafflement serves as the straight-man reaction beat — his honest confusion is funnier than any witty comeback
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man beat. Michael's refusal to even try to parse Tobias's statement is funnier than any engagement would be. Economy score is high — four words do exactly what's needed.

jokes.text: No one was buying me as a straight man. It was a little off-brand.
jokes.setup: Tobias explains why he's doing a character role rather than playing a straight-man type
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'straight man' refers simultaneously to acting (the straight-man/comic duo format) AND sexual orientation — reinforcing the show's running gag about his sexuality. 'Off-brand' treats his sexual identity as a product category.
jokes.explanation: One of the tightest Tobias double-entendres in the episode. 'Straight man' does perfect double duty, and 'off-brand' is inspired — it treats his queerness as a brand identity with complete sincerity. The economy is exceptional: five words carry the entire joke. Strong rewatch bonus because it's easy to miss the acting-terminology meaning on first pass.

jokes.text: What does Gwyneth Paltrow say? 'Consciously un-cuckold' me.
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to explain that Lindsay has ended their relationship
jokes.punchline: He mangles Gwyneth Paltrow's famous 'conscious uncoupling' phrase into 'consciously un-cuckold me' — which (a) doesn't mean what he thinks it means, (b) implies he accepts being a cuckold as his default status, and (c) the Paltrow reference is itself a joke
jokes.explanation: Multilayered and inventive. Tobias transforms the celebrity wellness buzzword into something that reveals his own degraded self-image. 'Un-cuckold' implies he accepts that cuckoldry was his baseline — the 'un' prefix doing heavy lifting. It's also a perfect Tobias malapropism because it sounds plausible as a Gwyneth Paltrow phrase. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Tobias's therapist mode: 'put my therapist leggings on for one second' followed by 'Sit, sit, sit, sit, sit, sit, sit.'
jokes.setup: Tobias offers to give Michael therapeutic advice and says he'll 'put his therapist leggings on.'
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'therapist leggings' (a absurd costume choice) followed by Tobias's manic repetition 'sit, sit, sit, sit, sit, sit, sit' as he tries to establish therapeutic authority — seven 'sits' — lands as perfect character absurdism.
jokes.explanation: 'Therapist leggings' is a wonderfully specific absurdity. The seven 'sits' is a physical comedy beat that implies Tobias performing therapeutic authority while Michael grows increasingly exasperated. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: Yes, yes, yes. I suppose I did... start too high. Okay, let's bring everyone down an octave.
jokes.setup: Michael refuses to engage with Tobias's dramatic therapeutic scene
jokes.punchline: Tobias treats the emotional moment as a musical performance — 'start too high' and 'bring everyone down an octave' — revealing he's directing a scene rather than having a human conversation
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment — Tobias's theatrical framing of human interaction is core to his character. 'Bring everyone down an octave' is a genuinely funny metaphor for emotional de-escalation.

jokes.text: I wish I had known you'd had such depth before I gave your part away to a less experienced actor.
jokes.setup: Michael opens up emotionally about his dead wife
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds to Michael's genuine emotional depth by lamenting that he gave 'Michael's part' to a lesser actor — he has cast Michael in his biopic-style theatrical project and is now casting-directing in real time
jokes.explanation: Excellent Tobias character comedy. His response to genuine grief is to critique the acting opportunity missed. 'A less experienced actor' implies Michael would be better at playing himself than whoever was cast — which is both absurd and a cutting dig at the replacement. The tonal whiplash from pathos to casting notes is perfectly AD.

jokes.text: I didn't know there was such a thing.
jokes.setup: Tobias says he gave Michael's part to 'a less experienced actor'
jokes.punchline: Michael deadpans that he didn't know a 'less experienced actor' was possible — implying Tobias himself is already at rock bottom
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical burn on Tobias. Michael's dry delivery makes it land as an effortless insult rather than a crafted one — which is exactly right for his character.

jokes.text: This is the simplest character in the entire family. He has one personality trait: he always comes back. Well, that and he always has his hands in his pockets.
jokes.setup: Tobias explains how he's preparing to play George Michael in the family ceremony
jokes.punchline: He reduces George Michael's character to two traits: (1) always coming back and (2) hands in pockets — the second being a weirdly specific physical tic elevated to personality-level significance
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Tobias's reductive acting analysis, and the show acknowledging George Michael's character arc (always returning). 'Hands in pockets' as a defining trait is a delightfully absurd specificity. Rewatch bonus because it's a meta-commentary on George Michael as a character.

jokes.text: You got peanuts in there?
jokes.setup: Tobias claims George Michael's signature trait is hands in pockets
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. immediately asks if he literally has peanuts in his pockets — treating the acting observation as a practical question
jokes.explanation: Quick G.O.B. non-sequitur that works through pure absurdist literalism. Short and effective.

jokes.text: There's enough of these for this to be a thing?
jokes.setup: George Michael discovers his father's scuba equipment / multiple instances of something unexpected
jokes.punchline: His baffled acknowledgment that there are enough of whatever he's found to constitute a recurring pattern — 'for this to be a thing' — is deadpan observational comedy
jokes.explanation: Mild observational beat. Works as a quick reaction but lacks specificity since the object is unclear from the transcript.

jokes.text: Looking for a grandparent? No, thank you. I... No, I have enough.
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at what appears to be a retirement community
jokes.punchline: He's asked if he's 'looking for a grandparent' — as if they're a commodity — and he reflexively declines before catching himself, then confirms he 'has enough' grandparents
jokes.explanation: The treating of grandparent-acquisition as a commodity transaction is a genuinely funny premise. 'I have enough' is the perfect George Michael response — polite refusal of something absurd as if it's a normal offer.

jokes.text: That's what the seniors do to keep the young people away. They don't want 'dot-commers' taking over.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks why the lobby of the senior center is so off-putting
jokes.punchline: Maeby explains the seniors deliberately make the lobby repellent as a defense mechanism against tech-era gentrification — 'dot-commers' — using the dated term as a joke about seniors' outdated perception of the enemy
jokes.explanation: A smart joke about seniors actively defending their space with the deliberate strategy of unpleasantness. 'Dot-commers' is a perfect choice — an already-dated term that signals the seniors' generational frame of reference. Solid observational comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I've become my father. ♪ In the most delicious way! ♪
jokes.setup: Maeby is disturbed to see herself aged in a gray wig, thinking 'I've become my father'
jokes.punchline: The expected horror of 'becoming your parent' is immediately undercut by her breaking into a joyful song fragment — she's delighted, not horrified, to resemble Lindsay in some way (inverted parental anxiety trope)
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is excellent — 'I've become my father' sets up horror, but 'In the most delicious way!' inverts it completely. Also functions as a meta-joke since Tobias is her father (not Lindsay), and there's gender play in 'my father' vs her female presentation. Rewatch bonus for the layered joke.

jokes.text: I didn't want to say anything, but I was like, 'Maeby looks like a 65-year-old.' What if I told you I was 75? Holy shit.
jokes.setup: George Michael admits Maeby's disguise looked genuinely old
jokes.punchline: Maeby claims to be 75 — even older than George Michael thought — and his shocked 'holy shit' treats this as actual news
jokes.explanation: Solid quick beat. The escalation from 65 to 75 and George Michael's genuine shock is funny. The makeup artistry joke lands through his credulity.

jokes.text: It's the teeth. It's the same guy who's doing my meth teeth, you know, for the family of the year thing.
jokes.setup: George Michael compliments Maeby's aged appearance
jokes.punchline: The teeth that make her look 75 are from the same makeup artist doing her 'meth teeth' for the family of the year ceremony — implying she's planning to show up to a family awards ceremony with drug-addict dental prosthetics
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Maeby has a 'meth teeth' set prepared for a family ceremony is wonderfully dark. The fact that this detail is delivered as incidental information — 'you know, for the family of the year thing' — as if this is completely routine is the key to why it works. High originality for the specific absurdity.

jokes.text: Where I write a speech for my mom, she talks about how great I am, then... Then you open your mouth and you're like, 'So why do I have to turn tricks for meth money?'
jokes.setup: Maeby explains the family of the year ceremony scam
jokes.punchline: George Michael completes her thought with the reveal that she'll be in meth-addict makeup — 'why do I have to turn tricks for meth money?' — completing the darkly absurdist picture of Lindsay accepting an award while Maeby looks like a drug casualty
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best sustained jokes. The image of Lindsay receiving a family of the year award while Maeby stands beside her with meth teeth and a 'tricks for meth money' speech is gloriously dark. George Michael completing the thought shows they've both internalized Maeby's scam logic. Strong dark subversive comedy.

jokes.text: I mean, when people catch on, they either forget or they die.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks how Maeby keeps her old-age scam going at a senior center
jokes.punchline: Her explanation of how scams stay uncaught at retirement communities: 'they either forget or they die' — delivered as casual business logic
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. 'They either forget or they die' is a perfectly constructed dark punchline — six words that reframe dementia and mortality as favorable business conditions. The deadpan delivery (implied by Maeby's character) makes the darkness land even harder. This is comparable to the 8+ anchor: highly quotable, maximum economy, completely original, and requires knowing Maeby's con-artist character to fully appreciate. It's the kind of line that gets repeated outside the show.

jokes.text: Of course, what is there to steal? A few barstools and some family pictures? Anyway, that's how I got those barstools and all these great pictures of grandkids.
jokes.setup: Maeby notes seniors don't lock their doors because they have nothing worth stealing
jokes.punchline: She immediately reveals she HAS stolen exactly those things — barstools and family photos — from her elderly neighbors
jokes.explanation: Textbook misdirection structure: setup dismisses the value of what's there, punchline reveals she took it anyway. 'Great pictures of grandkids' — as if they're valuable art — is the character-perfect touch. Maeby stealing sentimental items from the elderly is both dark and very funny.

jokes.text: What a beautiful family you've stolen.
jokes.setup: Maeby reveals she has other people's family photos on her walls
jokes.punchline: George Michael's deadpan compliment — 'what a beautiful family you've stolen' — treats the theft of sentimental items as a normal home décor situation
jokes.explanation: Short, perfectly constructed line. The complimentary format ('what a beautiful family') collides with 'you've stolen' to create a charming deadpan non-judgmental reaction to theft. One of the episode's most quotable short lines.

jokes.text: But whom Maeby did not. [Recognition of Stan Sitwell] Hello.
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces Stan Sitwell as someone 'longtime viewers will be mildly surprised to recognize'
jokes.punchline: The narrator's qualifier 'mildly surprised' is a meta-joke about the show's deep bench of recurring characters — calibrating viewer expectations with deliberate understatement
jokes.explanation: The narrator's self-aware calibration of viewer surprise is a meta-joke about the show's complexity. 'Mildly surprised' is exactly the right register for a deep-cut callback character reappearance. Rewards longtime viewers.

jokes.text: I am her. Sister. I am her sister. You're going the wrong way. Annette? Great idea. Do you think they have one? Have what? Annette. Yes, that's me.
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell says he's confused because the condo is listed as Lucille Austero's — Maeby needs to explain why she's there
jokes.punchline: Maeby improvises 'I am her sister' then hears someone call 'Annette' in the background and adopts it as her name — the joke is watching her con develop in real time through pure accident and quick thinking
jokes.explanation: Maeby improvising a con in real time is entertaining and character-consistent. The 'Annette' bit is chaotic and fun. Slightly bloated in execution but the energy carries it.

jokes.text: Lost all my personal paperwork in Hurricane Cantina.
jokes.setup: Maeby (as 'Annette') claims to have moved from New Orleans and needs to explain missing paperwork
jokes.punchline: She invents 'Hurricane Cantina' — a portmanteau of 'Hurricane Katrina' and 'cantina' (restaurant) — as the disaster that destroyed her documents
jokes.explanation: 'Hurricane Cantina' is an inspired portmanteau — close enough to Katrina to be plausible for a confused elderly man, but clearly a food establishment. The economy is exceptional: three words establish the lie and the joke simultaneously. It's the kind of wordplay that rewards a second listen.

jokes.text: Unless I'm expected to lose a home for the second time.
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell says the paperwork situation is complicated
jokes.punchline: Maeby — who has never actually owned a home — weaponizes fake victimhood about losing a home 'for the second time' to manipulate Stan's sympathy
jokes.explanation: Maeby's confident deployment of fake victimhood is consistent with her character. Competent con-artist comedy but not especially sharp.

jokes.text: Of course, I could just pack up my shorty shorts, my silky pajamas, my peekaboo bras, my ticklers, sex knobs, and lacy hoo-has and hit the road.
jokes.setup: Maeby threatens to leave the condo if Stan doesn't accept her claim
jokes.punchline: Her list of possessions to pack escalates from mild (shorty shorts, silky pajamas) to progressively more inappropriate (peekaboo bras, ticklers, sex knobs, lacy hoo-has) — the items belong to her sexually active elderly persona 'Annette'
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of a 'senior woman's' possessions is funny for the sheer escalation and the incongruity between age and content. 'Sex knobs' is an inspired made-up euphemism. The callback to George Michael's bag 'of lacy ticklers, hoo-ha bras, dog toys, and sex knobs' later connects this to Maeby's actual bag. Rewatch bonus for that callback.

jokes.text: Oh. He can't hear a thing. He's got horrible aids. Shoedini! The world's first shoe horn... Oh, hearing aids. Yeah, yeah, here they call them aids. The other ones they call aids.
jokes.setup: George Michael wonders why they're not whispering in front of the elderly man
jokes.punchline: Maeby says he 'has horrible aids' — George Michael (and the audience) initially processes this as HIV/AIDS, then 'Shoedini' ad plays, then clarification to 'hearing aids' — followed by Maeby's deadpan acknowledgment that both things are called 'aids'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly structured joke. The 'aids' misdirection is committed to fully — the Shoedini commercial interruption delays the clarification to maximum comedic effect. The final line 'the other ones they call aids' acknowledges the dark territory without flinching. The structure (setup → misdirection → clarification → re-acknowledgment of original reading) is textbook AD multi-layer construction.

jokes.text: Don't worry, if he asks who you are, I'll just tell him you're Tom Collins. He's always asking for a Tom Collins.
jokes.setup: George Michael is worried about being identified by the elderly man
jokes.punchline: Maeby will pass him off as 'Tom Collins' — a cocktail — because the man constantly requests the drink and can't tell the difference between a person and a drink order
jokes.explanation: Good joke about the cognitive decline of the elderly resident being exploited for cover. The Tom Collins cocktail as a cover identity is inspired — the joke implies the man is both confused and perpetually requesting cocktails.

jokes.text: Believe me, I'm in over my head enough as it is being George Maharis.
jokes.setup: Maeby is already operating under the alias of an elderly woman
jokes.punchline: 'George Maharis' is a near-homophone for 'George Maharis' (a real 1960s actor) but also sounds like 'George my Harris' — and more importantly, it's very close to 'George Michael' phonetically, suggesting Maeby created her cover name from George Michael's name
jokes.explanation: The name George Maharis is a real actor's name that also phonetically echoes George Michael — fitting for a show obsessed with wordplay and reference. Rewatch bonus for those who catch the real person reference.

jokes.text: You perverted that into a way to not hurt him. And then I had to turn down this scuba trip, which is also rude.
jokes.setup: Maeby explains to George Michael that she told him to say he was dating Rebel to protect his father
jokes.punchline: She frames George Michael's act of kindness as a perversion of her advice, then pivots to complaining that turning down scuba was 'also rude' — treating scuba trip rejection as a social faux pas equivalent to lying
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy. Maeby's moral calculus where scuba rudeness equals lie-related harm is funny but not especially crisp.

jokes.text: You passed out at the aquarium. They make you feel like you're underwater with all these giant... Dolphins? I think that was some kind of smiling, kind of bemused baby shark.
jokes.setup: Maeby says George Michael would have been scared of scuba anyway
jokes.punchline: He passed out at an aquarium — not even the ocean but a contained tourist attraction — and his explanation involves being overwhelmed by a 'bemused baby shark,' which he can only vaguely identify
jokes.explanation: 'Bemused baby shark' is an unexpectedly specific and charming description that elevates what could be a generic 'afraid of fish' joke. The image of a gentle, slightly confused small shark causing a fainting is endearingly absurd.

jokes.text: I don't know, things are volatile with my dad right now. It feels very combustible. Feels like we're on the edge of physical menace or something, which I've trained myself for, but has he been training, too?
jokes.setup: George Michael explains his anxiety about lying to his father
jokes.punchline: He frames his anxiety about a conversation with his dad as potential physical combat — 'edge of physical menace' — and his genuine concern about whether Michael has been physically training is played completely straight
jokes.explanation: The callback to the punch that defines the Michael/George Michael tension is well-deployed. George Michael's genuine strategic worry about his father's physical training regime is absurdly funny — he's treating a family dispute as a martial competition.

jokes.text: You go to Rebel's, you take my bag of lacy ticklers, hoo-ha bras, dog toys, and sex knobs with you. And you act like you're expecting a booty call, as the old people think the young people say.
jokes.setup: Maeby gives George Michael advice on how to handle the Rebel situation
jokes.punchline: She dispatches him with her elderly-disguise sex prop bag — which now includes 'dog toys' in the mix — and qualifies 'booty call' as something 'old people think the young people say,' acknowledging her own linguistic out-of-touchness
jokes.explanation: Callback to the prop bag from Maeby's earlier inventory. 'Dog toys' added to the list of sex props is a perfect absurdist detail. Maeby distancing herself from 'booty call' as old-person language while she IS old-person language is nicely self-aware.

jokes.text: You tell her, 'I'm here for a quickie, and I'm out of here.' Mr. Brash. And she'll drop you like a buttered cane.
jokes.setup: Maeby coaches George Michael on how to end things with Rebel
jokes.punchline: 'Drop you like a buttered cane' — she offers this as a contemporary slang expression, but it's clearly a senior-citizen-inflected version of 'drop you like a hot potato,' revealing she's absorbed elderly idioms into her fake persona so deeply they're now her actual idioms
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'Drop you like a buttered cane' is an inspired piece of comedy writing — it's the perfect melding of youth slang cadence ('drop you like a hot potato') with elderly imagery (a butter-greased walking cane). The implication that Maeby has so thoroughly inhabited her old-lady persona that she now thinks in senior idioms is both a character payoff and a genuinely funny original phrase. High originality because this exact joke cannot exist in any other show or context. The follow-up 'Something old people say? Yeah. I'm trying to get it going' perfectly seals it.

jokes.text: Something old people say? Yeah. I'm trying to get it going.
jokes.setup: 'Drop you like a buttered cane'
jokes.punchline: Maeby acknowledges it's not actual youth slang but tries to launch it as a genuine expression — treating her own accidental elderly contamination as a branding exercise
jokes.explanation: The acknowledgment that she's 'trying to get it going' — attempting to launch a senior idiom as youth slang — is a perfect character beat. It's Maeby treating her own assimilation of elderly speech patterns as an entrepreneurial opportunity.

jokes.text: George Michael's father had drifted farther south than he realized, despite swimming with a GPS system that cost roughly as much as Buster's left thumb.
jokes.setup: Michael is scuba diving and has drifted off course
jokes.punchline: The GPS cost 'roughly as much as Buster's left thumb' — a casual reference to Buster's prosthetic hand/arm (a major plot point and running gag), treating a traumatic amputation as a pricing metric
jokes.explanation: The narrator using Buster's prosthetic as a casual unit of currency is one of the show's best kinds of callback — it rewards deep familiarity while also working as pure dark absurdism. 'Roughly as much as Buster's left thumb' implies there is an actual known market value for Buster's hardware, which is both horrifying and funny.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael's father had drifted farther south than he realized, despite swimming with a GPS system that cost roughly as much as Buster's left thumb.'
jokes.setup: Michael is scuba diving, supposedly mapping the ocean floor.
jokes.punchline: The GPS's price is calibrated against the market value of Buster's severed left thumb — a casually dark unit of measurement.
jokes.explanation: This is a repeat of joke #21's construction — using Buster's thumb as a price unit. The repetition is intentional (the narrator is consistent in their calibration), which is itself an AD meta-joke about the show's internal consistency.

jokes.text: My wife died there. Ovarian cancer, actually. He's a liar. And... He shouldn't have said that. Ignore the man.
jokes.setup: Michael is looking at the beach cottage and tells a stranger his wife died there
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately calls Michael a liar about the ovarian cancer detail, then says 'he shouldn't have said that' and 'ignore the man' — retroactively calling out Michael for oversharing AND fabricating the cause of death as a stranger sympathy play
jokes.explanation: The narrator's editorial interjections calling Michael a liar mid-scene is a great use of the AD narrator format. The escalation from 'he's a liar' to 'ignore the man' is perfectly calibrated. The implicit reveal that Michael fabricated the cancer type is a dark character moment played for comedy.

jokes.text: Michael had just discovered his family never sold their family cottage. Lied to me, and that's worse.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers the cottage was never sold
jokes.punchline: The narrator — in Michael's voice — delivers 'and that's worse,' the episode's running gag phrase, applied to the narrator's own narration of Michael's reaction
jokes.explanation: A masterful use of the running gag. The narrator stepping into Michael's internal voice mid-narration and delivering 'and that's worse' — which we've been set up to expect — is the AD format at its best. The joke has been built to this moment and the payoff is satisfying. The economy (three extra words) does maximum work.

jokes.text: It was a horrible time for the housing market. Were we supposed to lose our precious daughter-in-law and a couple hundred grand in the same year?
jokes.setup: Michael asks why the family didn't sell the cottage after his wife died
jokes.punchline: Lucille frames not selling as financial prudence — equating the death of Michael's wife with a 'couple hundred grand' loss in the same sentence, treating grief and real estate as equivalent inconveniences
jokes.explanation: This is peak Lucille Bluth — reducing her son's wife's death to a line item alongside property values. 'Precious daughter-in-law' is delivered with the exact right amount of performative sentiment before pivoting to the financial consideration. Could only be Lucille. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Because we love you. And nobody wanted your feelings hurt. Mm. That was rule one. Rule one? It slipped down a little when we put in the new floors.
jokes.setup: The family had a 'rule one' about not hurting Michael's feelings regarding the cottage
jokes.punchline: The rule was apparently physically written down somewhere and literally slipped in position when new floors were installed — treating an emotional family agreement as a physical document that shifts with home renovation
jokes.explanation: The joke of a family rule literally being a physical document that 'slipped' with floor renovation is a pleasantly absurd detail. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: And by that point, you were already dating and in love with that total re... That totally lovely Rita.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains they didn't want to bring up bad memories once Michael started dating again
jokes.punchline: She nearly says 'total re[tard]' about Rita — a character from an earlier season who was intellectually disabled — then catches herself and pivots to 'totally lovely Rita,' revealing her true feelings about the character while feigning courtesy
jokes.explanation: A classic AD almost-callback. Lucille's self-correction is perfectly calibrated — the audience hears exactly what she almost said, she knows she almost said it, and the pivot to 'totally lovely' is so obviously forced that it's the joke. Strong character integration for Lucille's barely-concealed contempt.

jokes.text: My mother died here. G.O.B. was born here. And I'm sure you've heard, it was also your father's pad. Only from the next room. Please believe me, if I could turn back time, I never would've answered that door.
jokes.setup: Michael reflects on the bad memories the cottage holds
jokes.punchline: The escalating list — mother died, G.O.B. born — peaks at the revelation that George Sr. used it as his mistress pad, and Lucille reveals she was in the next room when he answered the door to said activities, ending with her wishing she'd never answered that door
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of cottage traumas works well — death, birth (of G.O.B., treated as equivalent to death), and infidelity. 'Please believe me, if I could turn back time, I never would've answered that door' is a lovely dark Lucille line about her own marriage.

jokes.text: H-Honestly, I never thought you'd get past the guard gate. I could barely get past them when Tobias and I got here.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Lucille was worried he'd find the cottage
jokes.punchline: She reveals she underestimated her own son's ability to navigate a guard gate — and adds that she herself barely made it past with Tobias (implying Tobias is so absurd that even legitimate visitors become suspicious)
jokes.explanation: Mild character joke at Tobias's expense. The implication that Tobias makes legitimate visitors suspicious is consistent with his character but not a sharp joke.

jokes.text: 'Please believe me, if I could turn back time, I never would've answered that door.' [re: George Sr.'s infidelity]
jokes.setup: Lucille reflects on overhearing George Sr.'s infidelity from the next room at the cottage.
jokes.punchline: 'If I could turn back time, I never would've answered that door' — Lucille's regret is not about the marriage, the affair, or her children: it's about answering a door.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's characteristically minimal emotional engagement — her life's greatest regret is answering a door, not the betrayal itself — is perfect character integration. 'I never would've answered that door' implies the pain came from knowledge, and ignorance would have been better: bleak, true, and very funny. 'I know' from Michael is the perfect understated response.

jokes.text: But I've already pinked away my sideburns to play Buster at the family of the year ceremony tonight. And Buster's already invited a date.
jokes.setup: Michael fires Tobias from the 'playing George Michael' role
jokes.punchline: Tobias has pivoted to playing BUSTER — and has already shaved his sideburns to do so ('pinked away') — and the Buster he's playing has already made social commitments
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Tobias has already shaved and committed to playing Buster — as if his fictional character has a social life to manage — is good AD absurdism. 'Pinked away' is a characteristically Tobias-esque verb.

jokes.text: Come out and meet your boyfriend, Lucille. Murph? Oh, I see you finally figured out how to use that contraption. I'm on the phone.
jokes.setup: Tobias, playing Buster, calls out his 'girlfriend' to meet the group
jokes.punchline: The girlfriend is another elderly woman playing along with the charade — and her response to Tobias is to treat his phone call as a real contraption confusion, and when told 'I'm on the phone,' she doesn't understand
jokes.explanation: Mild situational comedy. The scene is more setup than payoff here. The phone confusion is familiar comedy territory.

jokes.text: You are... I'm... A girl. You're a girl, Linds, uh, Murphy B... Lucille. Is he supposed to be me? Uh-uh-uh, Lucille Number 2. She's quite the grande dame. It's basically Michael without the peanut pockets.
jokes.setup: Chaos around who is playing whom in the Bluth family ceremony stand-ins
jokes.punchline: Tobias's characterization of Lucille Austero as 'basically Michael without the peanut pockets' — referencing the hands-in-pockets character tic established earlier, applied now to a 72-year-old woman
jokes.explanation: The 'peanut pockets' callback is elegant — it extends the earlier gag and applies it to an entirely different character, which makes it both a payoff and a new joke. The absurdity of defining Lucille Austero's character by absence of a tic is pleasingly convoluted.

jokes.text: Actually, sometimes, I think I want to be a dog doctor. Ow!
jokes.setup: Lucille says she doesn't think Tobias wants to be an actor
jokes.punchline: He agrees, pivoting to veterinary ambitions — 'dog doctor' — then immediately yelps in pain from some physical interaction
jokes.explanation: Tobias's alternate career aspirations are a running character bit. 'Dog doctor' is a charmingly naive term for veterinarian. The 'Ow!' physical beat adds physical comedy timing.

jokes.text: I know 50 gentlemen who would tuck their last nut to play this part.
jokes.setup: Lucille pressures Tobias to commit to the Lucille Austero role
jokes.punchline: 'Tuck their last nut' — a phrase that sounds like 'tuck their last nickel' (i.e., risk everything financially) but is instead anatomically specific in a way that changes the meaning entirely, delivered by Lucille with complete authority
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most memorable lines. Lucille's authority with the phrase makes it land harder. 'Tuck their last nut' works as a colloquialism that means something entirely different than it sounds — the double meaning (physical tucking vs. financial sacrifice) is compressed into four words of Lucille confidence. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: When you see him at the campaign event, you'll think you're looking at a 72-year-old pink mustachioed Lucille Austero.
jokes.setup: Lucille describes how convincing Tobias's Lucille Austero impression will be
jokes.punchline: The confidence with which she describes 'a 72-year-old pink mustachioed Lucille Austero' — acknowledging the mustache will be pink and visible — as a convincing disguise is the joke
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of Tobias with a pink mustachioed face playing a woman is established through dialogue description. Lucille's confidence in this as a convincing look is the character comedy layer.

jokes.text: We can't have Lucille Austero! [Echo] We can't have Lucille Austero. [Echo] She's running against Lindsay, for God's sake. [Echo] For God's sake.
jokes.setup: The plan to use Tobias as fake Lucille Austero is revealed
jokes.punchline: The repeated echo of 'We can't have Lucille Austero' creates an absurd verbal loop where multiple characters repeat each other's alarm — the joke is the theatrical repetition of an obvious point
jokes.explanation: The echo/repetition gag is a mild comedy device. Works as a quick beat but isn't especially crafted — the joke is purely structural (repetition) without much comic invention.

jokes.text: You're trying to teach me a lesson. You're trying to get even because I'm trying to teach... I don't know why you're trying to teach me a lesson, so lesson not learned, Mother.
jokes.setup: Michael suspects his mother of hiding Buster to teach him a lesson
jokes.punchline: He delivers a pre-emptive 'lesson not learned' speech — and realizes mid-sentence that he doesn't even know what lesson he'd supposedly be learning, yet commits to not learning it anyway
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-defeating logic delivered with conviction is a good character moment. The mid-sentence realization that he doesn't know the lesson yet still concluding 'lesson not learned' is absurdly funny.

jokes.text: Michael, did you do something for which we should be teaching you a lesson?
jokes.setup: Michael rants about Lucille teaching him lessons
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response reveals she hasn't been teaching him a lesson at all — she genuinely has no plan — and turns the tables by asking if he deserves one
jokes.explanation: Lucille's pivot to Michael's guilt is characteristically sharp. The implication that she's now interested in what lesson HE might need is a good Lucille power move.

jokes.text: Oh, someone told Michael. Does this mean we can start leaving towels on the floor?
jokes.setup: Michael discovers Buster is in the beach cottage
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s immediate response to the dramatic revelation is to ask about towel privileges — the 'no towels on the floor' was apparently a rule Michael instituted, and George Sr. has been living under this rule and resenting it
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflection joke. The emotional weight of 'Michael found out' is immediately defused by the mundane urgency of towel placement rules. George Sr.'s imprisoned-man excitement about towels is both funny and a character insight.

jokes.text: I'm making this into a trophy for Dad. I can't wait to see the one you got yourself.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is crafting a trophy for George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically says he can't wait to see the trophy G.O.B. made for himself — implying the obvious, that G.O.B. has made himself one too
jokes.explanation: Predictable but character-consistent joke. The implication that G.O.B. is self-awarding is obvious but gets a smile.

jokes.text: I'm the president or CEO or whatever of the Bluth Company. I control the company checkbook, so I don't need false things to make me feel important. Okay. There's no trophy for that. Although he did visit a trophy store earlier that day.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. claims he doesn't need false validation
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately reveals G.O.B. visited a trophy store that very day — completely undermining his declaration of self-sufficiency
jokes.explanation: A perfect use of the narrator format — immediate ironic undercut of character declaration. G.O.B.'s protestation is demolished in real time. The structure (bold claim → immediate narrator contradiction) is textbook AD and executed cleanly.

jokes.text: Everyone gets a trophy. I tell people to stay away from the business. It's always been a lousy business, and these days everybody shops online. Everybody gets a trophy.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. visits the trophy store and gets advice about the business
jokes.punchline: The trophy store owner — in a business literally built on individual achievement recognition — laments that 'everybody gets a trophy' has destroyed the meaning of trophies, while surrounded by the evidence of this fact
jokes.explanation: The irony of a trophy store owner lamenting trophy inflation is a solid observational joke. Competent commentary on participation trophy culture but not sharply written.

jokes.text: Well, that may be, but tell me this, how many people get a whole trophy store? You can't be serious. So the guy was like, 'Are you shitting me?' That's usually the sign that you've made yourself a great deal.
jokes.setup: The trophy store owner says the business is dying
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. has apparently purchased the entire trophy store — interpreting the owner's disbelief ('are you shitting me?') as a positive sign he got a great deal
jokes.explanation: G.O.B.'s logic that 'are you shitting me?' indicates a great deal is perfect G.O.B. — he interprets shock as validation. The purchase of an entire trophy store as a response to needing one trophy is gloriously disproportionate and character-perfect.

jokes.text: I said, 'I want you to find the biggest golden anchor you can find.' So they didn't have one of those? No, he didn't even have keys to the back room. Also, the heat doesn't turn off.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. explains his final trophy commission before buying the store
jokes.punchline: He wanted a golden anchor trophy — a specific and elaborate custom item — from a store whose owner didn't even have back room access, and the practical problem of an uncontrollable heating system is delivered as a parallel complaint of equal weight
jokes.explanation: The deadpan revelation that the heat doesn't turn off — stated as a simple fact alongside trophy logistics — is a good absurdist detail. The golden anchor as a trophy concept also plays nicely as a visual gag against the Bluth Company nautical themes.

jokes.text: This took a couple hours, so once I give this to Dad, then I'll have Joni Beard give me the deed to the trophy store.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. explains his plan with the trophy store
jokes.punchline: He purchased the trophy store and is now having someone give him the deed — as if the transaction requires a separate deed-transfer step, suggesting he's not entirely clear on how he bought it
jokes.explanation: Setup for future payoff rather than a standalone joke. Mild character comedy.

jokes.text: G.O.B., who hadn't been with a woman since he'd gotten closer to this man... I was wondering what you were going to pop out of. I mean this one... Vámonos. Thought that 'Busty' was referring to this woman...
jokes.setup: Michael asks G.O.B. if he's 'doing something with Busty' (referring to Buster)
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals G.O.B. interprets 'Busty' as a reference to the woman he's been with — except the narrator clarifies G.O.B. hasn't been with a woman since getting 'closer to this man' (Tony Wonder) — implying G.O.B.'s attraction to Tony is the real story
jokes.explanation: The misdirection between 'Busty' (Buster) and the woman works efficiently. The narrator's aside about G.O.B. not being with a woman 'since he'd gotten closer to this man' is the real joke — a characteristically oblique reference to G.O.B.'s developing feelings for Tony Wonder. Strong character integration for the season arc.

jokes.text: Where's the nitrous?
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is shown with a woman in what was the conference room
jokes.punchline: His first question upon being revealed is about nitrous oxide — the missing dental anesthetic from the old office — returning to the opening joke about the dental office and G.O.B.'s priorities
jokes.explanation: The callback to the nitrous question from the dental office scene is a nice structural loop. G.O.B.'s first concern upon being caught is the nitrous, not the situation itself — character-perfect priorities.

jokes.text: Got my answer. You really don't care about your children's feelings, do you? Oh, you're one to give parenting lessons. I hear I'm not the only one whose son has a nasty left hook.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille about G.O.B. (and implies she's hiding Buster)
jokes.punchline: Lucille deflects Michael's parenting criticism by referencing George Michael's punch — 'a nasty left hook' — which is both a callback and a pointed parental tu quoque
jokes.explanation: Lucille's parenting counter-attack is well-timed and character-consistent. 'Nasty left hook' is a casual-brutal characterization of the punch that reflects Lucille's unsentimental worldview.

jokes.text: You know, at least he did something that we have not been able to do. He stood up for himself when his father was wrong. In fact, it might be the thing that I'm most proud of him for. Hope he knows that. Uh, he definitely does not.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to frame George Michael's punch positively
jokes.punchline: He ends his heartfelt speech with 'Hope he knows that' — and Lucille's deadpan 'He definitely does not' immediately deflates the sentiment
jokes.explanation: The comedic whiplash between Michael's earnest hope and Lucille's flat certainty is well-timed. The joke is in the certainty — not 'probably not' but 'definitely does not.' Classic Lucille anti-sentiment.

jokes.text: Goodbye forever. See you tonight. See you tonight. See you tonight.
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically declares 'Goodbye forever' as he leaves
jokes.punchline: Every family member responds with 'See you tonight' — acknowledging they all know Michael's 'goodbye forever' declarations are meaningless, and he'll be at the family event tonight
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical callback. The triple 'See you tonight' from three different characters shows the entire family has collectively called Michael's bluff on his dramatic exits. Structure is excellent — the repetition builds the joke.

jokes.text: But his hopes for an emotional father-son reunion... Were ruined... By an emotional father-son reunion.
jokes.setup: Michael races to have an emotional moment with George Michael
jokes.punchline: The narrator's construction 'ruined by an emotional father-son reunion' — the thing that ruins the planned emotional reunion is another emotional reunion (George Sr. in George Michael's bed) — is a paradoxical joke about emotional overcrowding
jokes.explanation: The narrator's paradox construction is excellent writing — 'ruined by' the exact thing it was supposed to be is clean and clever. Economy is near-perfect. It's the AD narrator at his most wry.

jokes.text: What are you doing in George Michael's bed? Oh... just a little... just a little weep and sleep, you know.
jokes.setup: Michael finds George Sr. in George Michael's childhood bed, apparently crying
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s explanation — 'a little weep and sleep' — normalizes emotional breakdown napping as a casual activity, delivered with embarrassed but unashamed acceptance
jokes.explanation: 'Weep and sleep' is a perfectly coined phrase — the rhyme gives it instant memorability and the casualness with which George Sr. deploys it is characteristically self-aware. One of the episode's most quotable short phrases.

jokes.text: Want to be the big man, so... Can't do it at the office, obviously, and Lindsay's at the penthouse, and I can't go to the cot... Well, we don't... We only... we only have the penthouse, so I-I, uh, I came here.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains why he's weeping in a child's bed
jokes.punchline: He almost says 'cottage' then catches himself — revealing he almost referenced the cottage that was supposedly sold, before the self-correction that inadvertently reveals he knows about the cottage deception
jokes.explanation: A rewatch bonus joke — the stuttered 'can't go to the cot...' is the tell that George Sr. knows about the cottage. Easy to miss on first watch. Functional dramatic irony but not a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: I know about the cottage. I hated that rule. Okay. I hated both rules. Oh, yeah, the towels. How'd you get past the guard gate? I came from the water. I knew it. I told her. I told her, 'He's gonna take us from the beach.'
jokes.setup: Michael reveals he knows about the cottage and the rules
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s immediate pivot to towel grievances — 'I hated both rules. Oh yeah, the towels' — reveals the towel rule was a major source of suffering. Michael arriving 'from the water' is treated by George Sr. as the fulfillment of a prophecy.
jokes.explanation: Multiple callbacks converge. George Sr.'s towel grievances and 'I told her he'd come from the beach' have good character consistency. The running gag of the towel rule pays off modestly.

jokes.text: Because your mom is being courted, Michael. Someone is fawning all over her. I knew she had Buster. No. I knew it. This one's not Buster. I mean, I wish that guy was Buster. Him I could fight, you know? You see, this guy got past me.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is upset about Lucille's new suitor
jokes.punchline: 'I wish that guy was Buster. Him I could fight' — George Sr. rates Buster as a beatable romantic rival and is distressed that this new suitor is beyond his fighting abilities
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s threat assessment of Buster as an easily defeatable rival is a good character joke — it reveals both his competitiveness and his estimation of Buster's physical prowess (low). Functional and character-specific.

jokes.text: I knew I should've sold that property when Stacy died. Tracey. No, we would've lost a fortune if we sold when Tracey died. No, Stacy was the paralegal I used to bring out there who went into insulin shock at the very height of the market. Sad.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says he should have sold the cottage when 'Stacy died'
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects him with 'Tracey' (his mother's name), but George Sr. clarifies he meant Stacy — a paralegal mistress who died on the property — and his 'Sad.' is delivered as a brief, perfunctory epitaph
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode — possibly one of the best in the season. The construction is perfect: George Sr. appears to be speaking about Tracey (Michael's dead wife, sacred territory), Michael corrects him with wounded sensitivity, and then the reveal that he meant a different dead woman (a mistress who died of insulin shock 'at the very height of the market') recontextualizes everything. 'Sad.' delivered as a one-word epitaph for his dead mistress — followed immediately by pivoting back to market timing — is dark comedy at its finest. Every element is exceptional: originality (the mistress dying of insulin shock at market peak), structure (misdirection → correction → worse reveal), character integration (exactly George Sr.'s casual amorality), economy (the single 'Sad.' is the perfect full stop), earned vs cheap (the darkness comes from genuine character insight, not shock alone). This is the episode's clear standout and approaches the 9.5 calibration anchor.

jokes.text: Van Halen was in town this weekend rocking audiences. But some beachgoers are getting rocked in a different way by a group calling themselves the Newport Bay Boys.
jokes.setup: A news report contextualizes the Newport Bay Boys rock-throwing gang
jokes.punchline: The anchor uses 'rocking audiences' to pivot to people being 'rocked in a different way' — a hoary news-anchor pun on rock music vs. rock-throwing violence
jokes.explanation: This is deliberately bad — a parody of TV news punning. The joke IS the badness of the pun, used three times with different bands (Van Halen, Nirvana, 50 Cent) to escalate the archaic humor. Scored low on craft because the humor is in the deliberate cheapness, not in wit.

jokes.text: The news rock pun repeated three times with Van Halen → Nirvana → 50 Cent, spanning decades of music
jokes.setup: The news anchor's rock pun plays once with Van Halen
jokes.punchline: The same footage replays with Nirvana, then 50 Cent — showing the gang's history and the news station's dedication to the same exact pun across decades
jokes.explanation: The repetition of the news pun across different musical eras (Van Halen/80s, Nirvana/90s, 50 Cent/00s) is an AD structural joke elevated by the passage of time. The same footage and same pun used to establish the gang has been doing this for decades — and the news station has used the same awful pun each time — is genuinely clever. The joke escalates with each repetition rather than diminishing. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Hey! Get off my beach, you stupid seagulls.
jokes.setup: The Newport Bay Boys are established as defending their beach from interlopers
jokes.punchline: Dusty yells at seagulls — actual birds — with the same intensity used against human beachgoers, revealing the rock-throwing extends to wildlife
jokes.explanation: The joke is the completeness of his territorial rage — it extends to birds. Short, absurd, well-timed as a visual beat.

jokes.text: Care to join me for a glass of breakfast wine?
jokes.setup: Lucille spots the beach hippie after the seagull incident
jokes.punchline: 'Breakfast wine' — treating wine as a morning beverage equivalent to orange juice — is a classic Lucille Bluth line that encapsulates her alcoholic casual entitlement
jokes.explanation: 'Breakfast wine' is a perfectly Lucille phrase — it naturalizes morning drinking through the 'breakfast' modifier without any shame. Economy is near-perfect. High character integration because this line couldn't come from anyone else. A genuinely quotable Lucille moment.

jokes.text: And before long, they were in the first stages of a beautiful early October-late November relationship.
jokes.setup: Lucille and the beach hippie start spending time together
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes it as an 'early October-late November relationship' — a euphemism for May-December (young/old) that acknowledges both parties are in autumn/winter of life, making it an October-November rather than May-December romance
jokes.explanation: An excellent linguistic invention. 'Early October-late November' is an elegant subversion of 'May-December' — acknowledging that when both parties are old, the age-gap metaphor shifts to autumn months. Clever wordplay that requires a beat to fully process, which makes it rewarding. One of the episode's most clever lines.

jokes.text: I mean, he's attracted to her. He's attracted to her. I'm sorry. Why would he be attracted to her if he's blind? And how did he burn off his fingertips? I have no idea what you're talking about. I may have added the fingertips. I assume if he's blind, he's gotta feel her face. But you said you never saw him touch her face? I never said he was blind.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains that Lucille's suitor is attracted to her
jokes.punchline: Michael has invented a whole narrative where the man is blind AND has burned-off fingertips — details he apparently added himself — and George Sr. has to walk back the increasingly elaborate disability he did not describe
jokes.explanation: Michael inventing increasingly specific disabilities (blind AND no fingertips) for a rival he's never seen is a great character joke — his anxious mind elaborates details to explain the inexplicable. 'I may have added the fingertips' is the moment of self-aware confession that makes it land. 'I never said he was blind' is the perfect straight-man anchor. Escalation structure works well.

jokes.text: I just told my son that I broke up with the woman that he's seeing, and in retrospect, I don't know, maybe she did not hear 'I'm leaving' when I said, uh, 'I ain't going nowhere.'
jokes.setup: Michael reflects on his communication with Rebel
jokes.punchline: He realizes 'I ain't going nowhere' (a double negative meaning 'I am staying') is the exact opposite of 'I'm leaving' — he said the precise opposite of what he intended and is only now considering that this might have caused confusion
jokes.explanation: The linguistic comedy of Michael's double negative undermining his intended departure is well-constructed. 'She did not hear I'm leaving when I said I ain't going nowhere' is the kind of self-aware yet still-confused reasoning that characterizes Michael well.

jokes.text: Sounds like a 'goodbye forever' to me.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'I ain't going nowhere' as a potential farewell statement
jokes.punchline: George Sr. recognizes the pattern as another 'goodbye forever' — connecting it to Michael's running habit of theatrical exits that he never follows through on
jokes.explanation: Clean callback to the 'goodbye forever' running gag, applied to Michael's romantic communications. Works as a running gag payoff and a character insight simultaneously.

jokes.text: Right? But if she tells my son, uh, what I said, you know what he's gonna say. Uh, 'You lied, and that's worse.'
jokes.setup: Michael worries about George Michael finding out about the Rebel situation
jokes.punchline: George Sr. delivers 'and that's worse' — completing the running gag, but now in George Sr.'s mouth, showing the phrase has been absorbed into the family consciousness as Michael's verbal signature
jokes.explanation: The 'and that's worse' gag lands here with accumulating force — now George Sr. is quoting Michael's verbal tic back at him, confirming it's a known family pattern. The callback gag is fully established and this is a satisfying iteration.

jokes.text: Yeah, you know, they can't wait to say stuff like that.
jokes.setup: George Sr. quotes 'and that's worse' as George Michael's predicted response
jokes.punchline: Michael agrees — 'they can't wait to say stuff like that' — not realizing he IS the person who says 'stuff like that,' as evidenced by his father quoting it back to him
jokes.explanation: The irony of Michael complaining about 'they' saying 'and that's worse' — when HE is the origin of the phrase — is perfect lack-of-self-awareness comedy. Works best in conjunction with the entire running gag.

jokes.text: Yeah. I mean, at least it won't destroy her life like your mother did mine.
jokes.setup: Michael sympathizes with George Sr.'s romantic situation
jokes.punchline: George Sr. assures Michael his breakup with Rebel won't be devastating — then immediately uses it as a vehicle to complain about Lucille destroying HIS life, pivoting from comfort to self-pity in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Classic George Sr. move — turns comfort into self-pity. Functional character comedy but not especially sharp.

jokes.text: You gonna be okay here? I ain't going nowhere. Perfect.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Sr. will be okay as he leaves
jokes.punchline: George Sr. says 'I ain't going nowhere' — echoing Michael's earlier phrase about Rebel — and Michael's 'Perfect' treats the double negative as a satisfying answer, showing neither of them will leave anything
jokes.explanation: A tight structural callback that closes both the 'goodbye forever' gag and the 'I ain't going nowhere' thread simultaneously. 'Perfect' as Michael's satisfied response to a phrase that means its opposite is a clean button.

tmdb_overview: Gob acquires a new office for the company with a "premium view." Michael gets a shock when he returns to a place that holds a lot of memories.
insight: Character comedy drives 79-point episode with Tobias verbal tics and Michael's con schemes.
